news_summary: Efforts to combat stigma are supporting the safe reintegration of Ebola frontline workers in Uganda.
news_summary_fr: Les initiatives visant à lutter contre la stigmatisation favorisent la réinsertion en toute sécurité des professionnels de première ligne qui ont lutté contre Ebola en Ouganda.
news_summary_es: Las iniciativas para combatir el estigma están contribuyendo a la reintegración segura de los trabajadores de primera línea que luchan contra el ébola en Uganda.

articles.title: A safe return home: Tackling stigma on Uganda's Ebola frontline
articles.summary: As Uganda works to contain an Ebola outbreak that is also affecting neighbouring Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC), health workers are doing more than responding to a deadly virus. They are helping families navigate fear, misinformation and stigma.
articles.content:
According to the latest figures, more than 1,400 confirmed cases of Ebola Bundibugyo virus disease, including 350 deaths, have been reported in the two countries. More than 90 per cent of infections are concentrated in Ituri province in DRC, a major cross-border trading hub with Uganda – ravaged by years of armed conflict.

Calling Kampala

On a Sunday morning in Kampala, Dr Chris Opesen, an anthropologist with the World Health Organization (WHO), receives a call from local authorities shortly after 5.30am.

A woman suspected of having Ebola is preparing to return home, and he has been asked to help ensure her reintegration into the community is safe.

Three days earlier, Lilian* had presented with symptoms consistent with Ebola and was transferred to the Ebola isolation unit at Mulago Hospital for diagnosis and care.

Although the transfer was planned, it was nonetheless a distressing experience for her, her family and neighbours. Since then, everyone has been anxiously awaiting the final confirmatory test that will determine whether she can safely return home.

Over the past 36 hours, Dr Opesen has remained in regular contact with Lilian and her family, offering reassurance and guidance as anxiety in the community has grown.

“The anthropologist is the midfielder of the outbreak response,” he says, drawing on a football analogy. “I connect response teams with communities and deliver feedback, from community to response teams citing concerns, fears and grievances.”

Managing fear and misinformation

Together with colleagues from the Kampala Capital City Authority, Dr. Opesen convenes a community meeting with Lilian’s family and friends to address concerns ahead of her return.

What begins as a tense and emotional exchange, gradually shifts when Dr. Opesen proposes a more structured dialogue. The group agrees and elects a chairperson, a neighbour, and a secretary: Lilian’s sister, Angela*.

© UNICEF/Joseph Balikuddembe

As each person is given space to speak, the atmosphere slowly calms. “We appreciate you, because without you there would still be fear,” Angela says afterwards.

Later, Dr Opesen and his colleague Henry Bwire, a surveillance focal point with the Kampala Capital City Authority, accompany a neighbour to a local shop.

Lilian had asked them to speak to the shopkeeper, who had allegedly stigmatised her mother the previous day.

“Ebola is a disease that everyone fears,” Mr. Bwire explains. “Sigma can come through miscommunication and fear, both of which were present in this case. It was our role to bring Lilian back and clear up that miscommunication and reduce that fear in the community.”

After the discussion, the shopkeeper thanks the team and reassures them that Lilian will be treated with respect on her return.

Waiting for the all-clear

All that remains is the laboratory confirmation.

Both decide to wait on neutral ground rather than return home, positioning themselves between the hospital and Lilian’s neighbourhood. They remain in contact with the laboratory and family throughout the afternoon.

At around 6:30pm, the call comes: the result is negative for Ebola. Lilian had been treated for a bacterial infection and is well enough to return home.

© WFP/Daisy Masembe

A dignified return

Back at the house, family and neighbours gather in anticipation. Dr. Opesen arrives with a cake and bottles of water.

When Lilian finally arrives, escorted by a member of Uganda’s national Emergency Medical Team, she appears tired but relieved. The group listens as she recounts her experience and what recovery will mean for her.

“As a family, we appreciate you coming to the ground and community to talk to us, because stigma can be too much” she says. “Thank you for listening to and addressing our concerns. I hope this can be a learning experience.”

Lilian then cuts the cake and shares it with everyone gathered together.

“For me reintegration is supposed to be a celebration, especially when the evacuation did not go as planned,” says Chris. “I wanted to do something special for Lilian, and for her to serve people the slices and for them to eat what she has given them, to demonstrate her acceptance back into the community.”

A long day, a shared outcome

By 9pm, more than 15 hours after his day began, Dr. Opesen is finally on his way home. Despite the long hours, he describes the day as rewarding.

“My role in the response gives me satisfaction,” he says. “If I do my job well, I can make a difference and support WHO’s leadership on the frontline of a safe and dignified response.”

*Names have been changed to protect patient privacy
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a meaningful health intervention addressing Ebola outbreak containment while tackling stigma and misinformation in affected communities. It describes a tangible, positive impact on public health and social cohesion in a high-risk region, with detailed context on community reintegration efforts, making it an inspiring story of compassionate healthcare and social support.
articles.personality_title: Uganda’s health workers help families fight Ebola fear and stigma
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Uganda and the neighboring Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) are facing an outbreak of Ebola Bundibugyo virus disease. Over 1,400 cases and 350 deaths have been reported, mostly in the DRC’s Ituri province, a busy border area with Uganda.

**What happened** – In Kampala, Uganda’s capital, Dr. Chris Opesen from the World Health Organization helped a woman named Lilian return safely to her community after she was suspected of having Ebola. Lilian was taken to a hospital for testing and treatment. While waiting for test results, Dr. Opesen spoke with Lilian’s family and neighbors to reduce fear and clear up misunderstandings. He also helped resolve a conflict when a shopkeeper treated Lilian’s mother unfairly because of stigma. When tests showed Lilian did not have Ebola, Dr. Opesen organized a small celebration to welcome her back.

**Impact** – This story shows how health workers do more than treat disease—they also help communities understand and accept patients. By talking openly with families and neighbors, Dr. Opesen helped reduce fear and stop stigma, making it safer for people like Lilian to return home. This kind of care supports both health and social connections during outbreaks.

**What's next step** – Health teams will continue working closely with communities to manage fear and misinformation while treating Ebola cases. This approach may help future outbreak responses be more effective and compassionate.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Uganda’s health workers combine medical care with community support to safely reintegrate patients and reduce Ebola stigma.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les agents de santé ougandais aident les familles à combattre la peur et la stigmatisation liées à Ebola
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’Ouganda et la République démocratique du Congo (RDC) font face à une épidémie de la maladie à virus Ebola Bundibugyo. Plus de 1 400 cas et 350 décès ont été signalés, principalement dans la province d’Ituri en RDC, une zone frontalière très fréquentée avec l’Ouganda.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – À Kampala, la capitale de l’Ouganda, le Dr Chris Opesen de l’Organisation mondiale de la santé a aidé une femme nommée Lilian à revenir en toute sécurité dans sa communauté après qu’elle ait été suspectée d’avoir Ebola. Lilian a été hospitalisée pour des tests et un traitement. En attendant les résultats, le Dr Opesen a parlé avec la famille et les voisins de Lilian pour réduire la peur et clarifier les malentendus. Il a aussi aidé à résoudre un conflit avec un commerçant qui avait maltraité la mère de Lilian à cause de la stigmatisation. Quand les tests ont montré que Lilian n’avait pas Ebola, le Dr Opesen a organisé une petite fête pour son retour.

**Impact** – Cette histoire montre que les agents de santé font plus que soigner la maladie : ils aident aussi les communautés à comprendre et à accepter les malades. En parlant ouvertement avec les familles et voisins, le Dr Opesen a aidé à réduire la peur et à stopper la stigmatisation, rendant le retour à la maison plus sûr pour des personnes comme Lilian. Ce type d’accompagnement soutient à la fois la santé et les liens sociaux pendant les épidémies.

**Prochaine étape** – Les équipes de santé continueront de travailler étroitement avec les communautés pour gérer la peur et la désinformation tout en traitant les cas d’Ebola. Cette approche pourrait rendre les réponses aux futures épidémies plus efficaces et humaines.

**En une phrase** – Les agents de santé ougandais allient soins médicaux et soutien communautaire pour réintégrer en sécurité les patients et réduire la stigmatisation liée à Ebola.
articles.personality_title_es: Los trabajadores de salud de Uganda ayudan a las familias a combatir el miedo y el estigma del Ébola
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Uganda y la vecina República Democrática del Congo (RDC) enfrentan un brote del virus Ébola Bundibugyo. Se han reportado más de 1,400 casos y 350 muertes, principalmente en la provincia de Ituri en la RDC, un área fronteriza con mucho comercio con Uganda.

**Qué pasó** – En Kampala, la capital de Uganda, el doctor Chris Opesen de la Organización Mundial de la Salud ayudó a una mujer llamada Lilian a regresar de forma segura a su comunidad después de que se sospechara que tenía Ébola. Lilian fue llevada al hospital para pruebas y tratamiento. Mientras esperaba los resultados, el doctor Opesen habló con la familia y vecinos para reducir el miedo y aclarar malentendidos. También ayudó a resolver un problema cuando un comerciante trató mal a la madre de Lilian por el estigma. Cuando las pruebas mostraron que Lilian no tenía Ébola, el doctor organizó una pequeña celebración para su regreso.

**Impacto** – Esta historia muestra que los trabajadores de salud hacen más que tratar la enfermedad; también ayudan a las comunidades a entender y aceptar a los pacientes. Al hablar abiertamente con familias y vecinos, el doctor Opesen ayudó a reducir el miedo y el estigma, haciendo más seguro que personas como Lilian regresen a su hogar. Este tipo de apoyo fortalece tanto la salud como las relaciones sociales durante los brotes.

**Próximo paso** – Los equipos de salud seguirán trabajando de cerca con las comunidades para manejar el miedo y la desinformación mientras tratan los casos de Ébola. Este enfoque puede hacer que las respuestas futuras sean más efectivas y humanas.

**Frase clave** – Los trabajadores de salud de Uganda combinan atención médica con apoyo comunitario para reintegrar con seguridad a los pacientes y reducir el estigma del Ébola.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, protective circle formed by diverse, gently clasped hands surrounding a small, welcoming village house with a cake on the doorstep, set against soft, earthy tones symbolizing community support, healing, and the bridging of fear and stigma.
